card.intuition: The VC dimension of a hypothesis class H is the largest set of points that H can shatter (classify correctly in all 2ⁿ possible labellings). Generalization bound: with probability ≥ 1−δ, R(h) ≤ R̂(h) + O(√(VCdim·log(n)/n + log(1/δ)/n)). Higher VC dim = more complex model = more data needed for good generalisation.
card.analogy: A family of classifiers as cookie cutters: the VC dimension is the maximum number of cookies you can cut such that any subset can be marked differently by some cutter in the family. A line in 2D can shatter 3 points (any 3 labelling), so VC dim = 3. Circles can also shatter 3 but not 4 specific configurations.
card.time_complexity: O(2^n) to check shattering for all labellings of n points
when_to_use: Bounding generalisation error — higher VC dim requires more data
Comparing model complexity: linear classifier VC=d+1, k-NN VC=∞
Structural risk minimisation: choose model with best bias-variance tradeoff
gotchas: VC dimension is about worst-case shattering, not typical performance
Neural networks have finite but very large VC dimension — bounds are loose in practice
PAC learning requires VC dimension to be finite for distribution-free generalisation

exercise.prompt: Check if a set of points can be shattered by axis-aligned rectangles in 2D.
exercise.starter_code:
def can_shatter_with_rectangle(points, labels):
    """
    Check if there exists an axis-aligned rectangle that classifies
    points with the given binary labels (1=inside, 0=outside).
    points: list of (x,y)
    labels: list of 0/1
    """
    positives = [p for p, l in zip(points, labels) if l == 1]
    negatives = [p for p, l in zip(points, labels) if l == 0]
    if not positives:
        return True   # empty rectangle works
    # Tightest bounding box around positives
    x_min = min(p[0] for p in positives)
    x_max = max(p[0] for p in positives)
    y_min = min(p[1] for p in positives)
    y_max = max(p[1] for p in positives)
    # Check no negative falls inside
    for nx, ny in negatives:
        if ___________:
            return False
    return True
exercise.solution: x_min <= nx <= x_max and y_min <= ny <= y_max
hints: A negative point invalidates the rectangle if it falls strictly inside the bounding box of all positive points.
exercise.test_code:
pts = [(0,0),(1,0),(0,1),(1,1)]  # 4 corners

# Label corners {00,11}=1, {01,10}=0 (XOR — cannot shatter with rectangle)
assert not can_shatter_with_rectangle(pts, [1,0,0,1])

# Label top-right corner only = 1 (rectangle around one point)
assert can_shatter_with_rectangle(pts, [0,0,0,1])

# All positive: large bounding box
assert can_shatter_with_rectangle(pts, [1,1,1,1])

# All negative: empty rectangle
assert can_shatter_with_rectangle(pts, [0,0,0,0])
print('PASS')
